title: भाग्य सूक्तम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री भाग्य सूक्तम् ॥

ॐ तच्छं योरावृणीमहे। गातुं यज्ञाय। गातुं यज्ञपतये। दैवी स्वस्तिरस्तु नः।
स्वस्तिर्मानुषेभ्यः। ऊर्ध्वं जिगातु भेषजम्। शं नो अस्तु द्विपदे। शं चतुष्पदे।
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥

प्रातरग्निं प्रातरिन्द्रं हवामहे प्रातर्मित्रावरुणा प्रातरश्विना।
प्रातर्भगं पूषणं ब्रह्मणस्पतिं प्रातः सोममुत रुद्रं हुवेम॥ १॥

प्रातर्जितं भगमुग्रं हुवेम वयं पुत्रमदितेर्यो विधर्ता।
आध्रश्चिद्यं मन्यमानस्तुरश्चिद्राजा चिद्यं भगं भक्षीत्याह॥ २॥

भग प्रणेतर्भग सत्यराधो भगेमां धियमुदवा ददन्नः।
भग प्र णो जनय गोभिरश्वैर्भग प्र नृभिर्नृवन्तः स्याम॥ ३॥

उतेदानीं भगवन्तः स्यामोत प्रपित्व उत मध्ये अह्नाम्।
उतोदिता मघवन्सूर्यस्य वयं देवानां सुमतौ स्याम॥ ४॥

भग एव भगवान् अस्तु देवास्तेन वयं भगवन्तः स्याम।
तं त्वा भग सर्व इज्जोहवीमि स नो भग पुरएता भवेह॥ ५॥

समध्वरायोषसो नमन्त दधिक्रावेव शुचये पदाय।
अर्वाचीनं वसुविदं भगं नो रथमिवाश्वा वाजिन आ वहन्तु॥ ६॥

अश्वावतीर्गोमतीर्न उषासो वीरवतीः सदमुच्छन्तु भद्राः।
घृतं दुहाना विश्वतः प्रपीता यूयं पात स्वस्तिभिः सदा नः॥ ७॥

ॐ तच्छं योरावृणीमहे। गातुं यज्ञाय। गातुं यज्ञपतये। दैवी स्वस्तिरस्तु नः।
स्वस्तिर्मानुषेभ्यः। ऊर्ध्वं जिगातु भेषजम्। शं नो अस्तु द्विपदे। शं चतुष्पदे।
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Bhagya Suktam ||

Om Tachchham yoravrinmahe | Gatum yajnaya | Gatum yajnapataye | Daivi svastirastu nah |
Svastir-manushebhyah | Urdhvam jigatu bheshajam | Sham no astu dvipade | Sham chatushpade |
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih ||

Pratar-agnim pratar-Indram havamaha pratar-Mitravaruna pratar-Ashvina |
Pratar-Bhagam Pushanam Brahmanaspatim pratah Somam-uta Rudram huvema || 1 ||

Pratar-jitam Bhagam-ugram huvema vayam putram-Aditer-yo vidharta |
Adhrash-chid-yam manyamanash-turash-chid-raja chid-yam Bhagam bhakshity-aha || 2 ||

Bhaga pranet-ar-Bhaga satya-radho Bhage-mam dhiyam-udava dadannaha |
Bhaga pra no janaya gobhir-ashvair-Bhaga pra nribhir-nrivantah syama || 3 ||

Uted-anim bhagavantah syamo-ta prapitva uta madhye ahnam |
Utodita Maghavan-Suryasya vayam devanam sumatau syama || 4 ||

Bhaga eva Bhagavan astu deva-stena vayam bhagavantah syama |
Tam tva Bhaga sarva ij-johavimi sa no Bhaga pura-eta bhaveha || 5 ||

Sam-adhvarayo-shaso namanta dadhikraveva shuchaye padaya |
Arvachinam vasuvidam Bhagam no ratham-iva-ashva vajina a vahantu || 6 ||

Ashvavatir-gomatir-na ushaso viravaith sadamuchchhantu bhadrah |
Ghritam duhana vishvatah prapita yuyam pata svastibhih sada nah || 7 ||

Om Tachchham yoravrinmahe | Gatum yajnaya | Gatum yajnapataye | Daivi svastirastu nah |
Svastir-manushebhyah | Urdhvam jigatu bheshajam | Sham no astu dvipade | Sham chatushpade |
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih ||
hindiMeaning:
प्रातःकाल अग्नि को, इन्द्र को बुलाते हैं, प्रातःकाल मित्र-वरुण को, अश्विनीकुमारों को। प्रातःकाल भग को, पूषा को, ब्रह्मणस्पति को, प्रातःकाल सोम और रुद्र को बुलाएँ॥ १॥

प्रातःकाल विजयी, उग्र भग को बुलाते हैं, जो अदिति के पुत्र और विधाता हैं। निर्धन भी, मानने वाला भी, शीघ्रगामी भी, राजा भी कहता है — मुझे भग (भाग्य) का भोग मिले॥ २॥

हे भग! नेता, सत्य प्रदान करने वाले, हमें यह बुद्धि ऊपर उठाकर दो। हे भग! हमें गौ, अश्व से सम्पन्न करो। हे भग! हम पुरुषों से समृद्ध (वीरवान) हों॥ ३॥

अभी भी हम भाग्यवान हों, सायंकाल भी, दोपहर में भी। हे मघवन! सूर्य के उदय होने पर भी हम देवताओं की सुमति (कृपा) में रहें॥ ४॥

भग ही भगवान हों, हे देवताओं! उनसे हम भगवान (भाग्यशाली) हों। हे भग! सब तुम्हें पुकारते हैं — तुम हमारे अगुवा बनो यहाँ॥ ५॥

अश्वों से युक्त, गायों से युक्त, वीरों से युक्त ऊषाएँ सदैव कल्याणमयी उदित हों। चारों ओर से घृत दुहती हुई, तुम सदा स्वस्ति से हमारी रक्षा करो॥ ७॥
benefits: Powerful Vedic hymn dedicated to Bhaga, the deity of fortune
Attracts good luck, fortune, and favorable destiny
Invokes wealth in the form of cattle, horses, and resources
Ideal for chanting at dawn (pratar) for daily blessings
Invokes multiple Vedic deities for all-round prosperity
Traditionally recited for removal of ill fortune and establishes auspiciousness
Makes the devotee Bhagavan (possessor of divine fortune)
description: Bhagya Suktam (भाग्य सूक्तम्) is a Vedic hymn dedicated to Lord Vishnu. Attributed to Rigveda (7.41), this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Devotees recite this prayer to grant wealth in the form of cattle, horses, and resources and invoke multiple Vedic deities for all-round prosperity. It is also said to remove ill fortune and establish auspiciousness. This sacred text is ideally recited on Sunday (Ravivar) and Thursday (Guruvar) for enhanced blessings. Comprising 7 verses, it can be completed in approximately 6 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagya Suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Bhagya Suktam and where is it preserved?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhagya Suktam (भाग्य सूक्तम्) — literally 'the hymn of good fortune' — is a beautiful Vedic hymn of seven verses dedicated to Bhaga, the Vedic deva of bhagya (fortune, destiny, divinely-allotted share, prosperity and rightful inheritance). The hymn is preserved in the Rigveda — Mandala 7, Sukta 41 — composed by the great rishi Vasishtha and attributed to the Bhaga devata. It is traditionally chanted with the Vedic shanti-mantra 'Tachcham yor avrinmahe' as opening and closing benediction. The Bhagya Suktam invokes a constellation of dawn-deities — Agni, Indra, Mitra-Varuna, Ashvinau, Bhaga, Pushan, Brahmanaspati, Soma, Rudra and Ushas — in the early-morning prayer (pratar) for the day's auspicious destiny, victory, prosperity and divine favour. It is one of the most powerful Vedic mantras for attracting good fortune and is widely recited in homa, sandhya and pratah-japa. (Sources: Rigveda 7.41; classical Vedic recitation tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Bhaga in the Vedic pantheon?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhaga is one of the twelve Adityas (sons of Aditi, the eternal mother and Kashyapa) in the Vedic pantheon — the brothers of Indra, Mitra, Varuna, Aryaman, Daksha, Amshu, Vivasvat, Tvashtra, Pushan, Vishnu, Savita and Dhata. As the deva of fortune, his name itself means 'allotter, dispenser, share, fortune' — Bhaga is the cosmic principle that distributes the divinely-decreed share of prosperity, wealth, marital happiness and rightful destiny to each being. He is invoked particularly at dawn (pratar) because the rising sun marks the beginning of the day's allocation of fortune. Bhaga is the etymological root of bhagya (fortune), bhagavan (the fortunate one, the lord), bhakti (sharing, devotion) and saubhagya (good fortune, especially marital). He is also associated with marriage and conjugal fortune — many Vedic marriage mantras invoke Bhaga for the bride's saubhagya. (Sources: Rigveda 7.41 and 1.136; Brahmanas; classical Vedic theology.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous Pratar Agnim Indram Bhagam mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse of the Bhagya Suktam — 'Pratar Agnim, pratar Indram havamahe, pratar Mitra-Varuna pratar Ashvina, pratar Bhagam Pushanam Brahmanaspatim, pratah Somam-uta Rudram huvema' — is the supreme Vedic morning invocation. Word-by-word: Pratah — at dawn, in the early morning; Agnim — Agni, the sacred fire; Indram — Indra, the king of devas; havamahe — we invoke (call to us); pratar Mitra-Varuna — at dawn (we invoke) Mitra and Varuna; pratar Ashvina — at dawn (we invoke) the Ashwini twins; pratar Bhagam — at dawn Bhaga; Pushanam — Pushan (the nourisher); Brahmanaspatim — Brihaspati (lord of prayer); pratah Somam — at dawn Soma; uta Rudram huvema — and Rudra (Shiva) we invoke. The mantra is a complete morning sankalpa, calling forth ten principal devatas at the moment of pratah-sandhya to bless the day. The repetition of 'pratah' (at dawn) emphasises that early-morning prayer is the supreme time to attract divine fortune. (Sources: Rigveda 7.41.1; classical Vedic morning prayer.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Bhaga eva Bhagavan astu?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 5 of the Bhagya Suktam — 'Bhaga eva Bhagavan astu devas tena vayam bhagavantah syama, tam tva Bhaga sarva ij-johavimi sa no Bhaga pura-eta bhaveha' — is one of the most philosophically profound declarations in the entire Veda. Word-by-word: Bhaga eva Bhagavan astu — let Bhaga himself truly be Bhagavan (the supreme divine); devah — O devas; tena vayam bhagavantah syama — by him may we become bhagavan (fortunate ones, those possessing bhaga); tam tva Bhaga sarva it johavimi — to you, O Bhaga, all (devotees) repeatedly call out; sa nah Bhaga pura-eta bhaveha — may you, O Bhaga, become our leader (pura-eta) here. The verse declares that Bhaga is itself Bhagavan — the supreme divine — and that to attain true fortune is to become bhagavan, a sharer in divine grace. The word 'Bhagavan' for the supreme Lord (used for Vishnu, Shiva, Krishna, Buddha, Mahavira) etymologically derives from this Bhaga of the Bhagya Suktam. (Sources: Rigveda 7.41.5; classical Vedic theology.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does regular Bhagya Suktam recitation bring?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional Vedic teachers attribute the following benefits to regular pratah (early-morning) Bhagya Suktam recitation: attraction of bhagya (good fortune) and saubhagya (especially marital fortune, success in finding the right spouse, longevity of the spouse); prosperity in the form of cattle, horses, vehicles, gold and worldly resources (verse 3 — 'Bhaga pra no janaya gobhir-ashvair Bhaga pra nribhir nrivantah syama'); success in career, business and rightful inheritance (since Bhaga is the deva who distributes the rightful share); favour from authorities, courts and powerful persons; removal of dur-bhagya (ill fortune) and obstacles caused by past karma; harmony in the family with all members blessed by Bhaga; the supreme phala of becoming bhagavan — a sharer in divine fortune; planetary remedy for weak Sun (Surya) and afflicted Venus (Shukra) in the natal chart. Pratah recitation in the brahma-muhurta is especially powerful. (Sources: Bhagya Suktam phala-tradition; classical Vedic teaching.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Bhagya Suktam differ from the Sri Suktam?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are Vedic hymns for prosperity and fortune but differ in deity, source and emphasis. The Sri Suktam is preserved in the Rigveda Khila (Khilani 2.6 / 5.87) and is addressed to Devi Sri Lakshmi — the goddess of wealth, beauty, prosperity and grace — invoked through Agni-Jatavedah to come and dwell with the devotee. Its 15-16 mantras emphasise dhana (wealth), dhanya (grain), gomata (cows), and the destruction of Alakshmi (inauspiciousness). The Bhagya Suktam is from the canonical Rigveda samhita (Mandala 7.41) by Maharshi Vasishtha, addressed to the Aditya Bhaga — the deva of fortune and divinely-allotted share — invoked along with a constellation of ten dawn-deities. Its 7 mantras emphasise saubhagya (especially marital), bhagavata (becoming a sharer in divine fortune), and morning-blessing. Tradition recommends both for comprehensive prosperity sadhana — Sri Suktam for material wealth via Lakshmi, Bhagya Suktam for destiny-favour via Bhaga. (Sources: Rigveda 7.41; Rigveda Khila 2.6; classical Vedic prosperity-mantra tradition.)
